questions.question:
Suppose the predicate F(x, y, t) is used to represent the statement that person x can fool person y at time t. Which one of the statements below expresses best the meaning of the formula,
~(forall x)(exists y)(forall t) F(x, y, t)

A. Everyone can fool some person at some time
B. No one can fool everyone all the time
C. Everyone cannot fool some person all the time
D. No one can fool some person at some time
questions.answer: B. No one can fool everyone all the time
questions.to_find: Natural language meaning of ~(forall x)(exists y)(forall t) F(x,y,t)

understand.plain: The inner formula (forall x)(exists y)(forall t) F(x,y,t) says 'for every person x, there exists some person y such that x can fool y at all times', i.e., everyone can fool someone all the time. Negating this gives: it is NOT the case that everyone can fool some person all the time — equivalently, there is at least one person who cannot fool every other person forever. More simply: no one can fool everyone all the time.

keywords.explain: ~(forall x) P(x) is equivalent to (exists x) ~P(x) — the negation of 'for all' is 'there exists a counterexample'.
keywords.example: ~(forall x)(mortal(x)) means 'some x is not mortal'

keywords.explain: When quantifiers are nested, work from outside in: each quantifier binds its variable for the scope of all inner quantifiers.
keywords.example: (forall x)(exists y) loves(x,y) means 'everyone loves someone (possibly different for each x)'

understand.visual_alt: The negation pushes inside the nested quantifiers, flipping forall to exists and exists to forall, yielding: there exists x such that for all y there exists t such that ~F(x,y,t), meaning some person cannot fool everyone at all times.

given.aim: Determine the natural language meaning of the negation of the statement 'every person can fool some person at all times'.

terms.meaning: Person x can fool person y at time t
terms.example: F(Alice, Bob, morning) = Alice can fool Bob in the morning
terms.connects: This is the base atomic predicate of the formula

terms.meaning: Every person x has some person y they can fool at every time t
terms.example: Everyone can fool at least one specific person at all times
terms.connects: This is the formula whose negation we must interpret

terms.meaning: The outer ~ negates the entire quantified statement
terms.connects: Applying De Morgan's law for quantifiers repeatedly changes the meaning

given.plan: Apply quantifier negation laws step by step: ~(forall x) becomes (exists x), ~(exists y) becomes (forall y), ~(forall t) becomes (exists t), and F becomes ~F. Then read the resulting formula in plain English.

steps.title: Apply quantifier negation laws
steps.formula_raw: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x);  ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)
steps.apply:
~(forall x)(exists y)(forall t) F(x,y,t)
= (exists x) ~(exists y)(forall t) F(x,y,t)
= (exists x)(forall y) ~(forall t) F(x,y,t)
= (exists x)(forall y)(exists t) ~F(x,y,t)
steps.note: Each negation flips the quantifier: forall <-> exists. The predicate F finally gets negated.

steps.title: Read the resulting formula in English
steps.apply: There exists a person x such that for every person y there is some time t when x cannot fool y. In other words, there is some person who cannot fool everyone at all times — equivalently, no one can fool everyone all the time.
steps.note: Option B captures this exactly. Options A, C, D misread the quantifier structure.

solution.result: B. No one can fool everyone all the time

questions.formula_note: Quantifier negation (De Morgan for quantifiers) is key: negating a universally quantified statement yields an existentially quantified negation, and this must be applied at each level of nesting.

questions.question:
Which one of the following options is CORRECT given three positive integers x, y, and z, and a predicate
P(x) = ~(x = 1) ^ (forall y)(forall z)((x = y * z) -> (y = 1 v z = 1))

A. P(x) being true means that x is a prime number
B. P(x) being true means that x is a number other than 1
C. P(x) is always true irrespective of the value of x
D. P(x) being true means that x has exactly two factors other than 1 and x
questions.answer: A. P(x) being true means that x is a prime number
questions.to_find: The natural language interpretation of the predicate P(x)

understand.plain: The predicate P(x) has two parts joined by conjunction. The first part ~(x=1) rules out x=1. The second part says: for every y and z, if x equals y*z, then either y=1 or z=1. This means x has no non-trivial factorization — it cannot be written as a product of two numbers both greater than 1. That is exactly the definition of a prime number.

keywords.explain: A positive integer p > 1 is prime if its only divisors are 1 and itself; equivalently, if p = y*z with positive integers y, z, then y=1 or z=1.
keywords.example: 5 is prime: 5=1*5 only, so any factorization forces one factor to be 1

keywords.explain: (forall y)(forall z)((x = y*z) -> (y=1 v z=1)) translates to 'for every factorization of x, one factor must be 1'.
keywords.example: For x=6, y=2, z=3 gives 6=2*3 but neither y=1 nor z=1, so P(6) is false — 6 is not prime

understand.visual_alt: P(x) checks that x is not 1 AND that every way to write x as a product y*z forces one of y, z to be 1 — the classic primality definition.

given.aim: Determine what mathematical property P(x) describes for positive integer x.

terms.meaning: x is not equal to 1; excludes the unit from consideration
terms.example: Without this clause, 1 would vacuously satisfy the second part
terms.connects: Necessary because primes are defined for integers greater than 1

terms.meaning: Every factorization of x forces at least one factor to be 1
terms.example: For x=7: only factorizations are 1*7 and 7*1, both with a factor of 1
terms.connects: This is the universal definition of irreducibility / primality

terms.meaning: y and z are factors of x
terms.example: x=12: y=3, z=4 is a factorization; neither is 1, so P(12) is false
terms.connects: The implication checks whether every such pair satisfies the constraint

given.plan: Unpack the conjunction: x != 1 AND (every factorization of x has a trivial factor). Recognize that this is the classical definition of a prime number, then verify against each option.

steps.title: Parse the conjunction
steps.apply:
P(x) = [x != 1] AND [for all y,z: if x=y*z then y=1 or z=1].
First clause: x is greater than 1 (positive integers only, so x >= 2).
Second clause: x cannot be factored into two numbers both > 1.
steps.note: Option D claims 'two factors other than 1 and x' — that would describe numbers with exactly one non-trivial factor pair, but option A is the correct interpretation of irreducibility.

steps.title: Match to definition of prime
steps.formula_raw: p is prime iff p > 1 and for all a,b: p = a*b => a=1 or b=1
steps.apply: P(x) is true iff x >= 2 (x != 1 in positive integers) AND every factorization of x is trivial (one factor is 1). This is precisely the definition of a prime number. Check: P(6) is false because 6=2*3 with neither factor 1. P(7) is true because 7=1*7 is the only factorization.
steps.note: Option A is correct. Option D is wrong: a prime p has factors 1 and p; that IS two factors, but the predicate doesn't count factors — it prevents non-trivial factorizations.

solution.result: A. P(x) being true means that x is a prime number

questions.formula_note: The universal-conditional pattern (forall y,z: x=y*z -> y=1 or z=1) captures the formal definition of primality — no non-trivial factorization exists.

questions.question:
What is the correct translation of the following statement into mathematical logic?
"Some real numbers are rational"

A. (forall x)(real(x) -> rational(x))
B. (forall x)(real(x) ^ rational(x))
C. (exists x)(real(x) ^ rational(x))
D. (exists x)(real(x) -> rational(x))
questions.to_find: FOL translation of 'Some real numbers are rational'

understand.plain: The word 'some' signals an existential quantifier. The statement 'some real number is rational' means there exists at least one thing that is both real AND rational. The key is that existential statements use conjunction (^), not implication (->). Using -> with 'exists' would be a classic trap: (exists x)(real(x) -> rational(x)) is trivially true if any non-real thing exists, because the implication is vacuously satisfied.

keywords.explain: 'Some A has property B' translates to (exists x)(A(x) ^ B(x)) — x must be BOTH an A and have property B.
(exists x)(A(x) -> B(x)) is NOT 'some A is B'; it is trivially true whenever some x with ~A(x) exists.
keywords.example: If Bob is not a student, then (exists x)(student(x) -> passes(x)) is true via Bob — vacuously

understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram with circle R (reals) and circle Q (rationals) overlapping; 'some real is rational' means the overlap region is non-empty, captured by (exists x)(real(x) ^ rational(x)).

given.aim: Translate the English statement 'Some real numbers are rational' into a well-formed FOL formula.

terms.connects: Restricts the domain property of the entity being considered

terms.meaning: x is a rational number (expressible as p/q, integers p and q, q != 0)
terms.connects: The second property the entity must possess

terms.meaning: Indicates existence of at least one member satisfying the condition
terms.connects: Determines use of exists quantifier with conjunction

given.plan: Identify 'some' as an existential quantifier, then join the two properties with conjunction (^) because the entity must satisfy both simultaneously.

steps.title: Identify quantifier and connective
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply:
'Some real numbers are rational' -> there exists an x such that x is real AND x is rational.
Formula: (exists x)(real(x) ^ rational(x)).
steps.note: The existence of rational numbers (like 1/2) that are also real confirms this is non-vacuously true.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply:
A uses forall with ->, which means 'all reals are rational' — false (pi is real but irrational).
B uses forall with ^, which claims everything in the domain is both real and rational — false.
D uses exists with ->, which is logically different and would be vacuously true for non-reals.
Only C, (exists x)(real(x) ^ rational(x)), correctly captures 'some real is rational'.
steps.note: The exists-conjunction pattern is the correct form for 'some A are B' in FOL.

questions.formula_note: The existential-conjunction rule is key: 'some A are B' always translates to (exists x)(A(x) ^ B(x)); using implication with exists is a common but incorrect trap.

questions.question:
What is the logical translation of the following statement?
"None of my friends are perfect."

A. (exists x)(F(x) ^ ~P(x))
B. (exists x)(F(x) -> ~P(x))
C. ~(exists x)(F(x) ^ P(x))
D. (forall x)(F(x) -> ~P(x))
questions.to_find: FOL translation of 'None of my friends are perfect'

understand.plain: 'None of my friends are perfect' is a universal claim: for every person, IF they are my friend, then they are NOT perfect. This universally rules out any friend being perfect. The phrase 'none are B' means 'all are not B', which in FOL is (forall x)(A(x) -> ~B(x)). Note that option C (~(exists x)(F(x) ^ P(x))) is logically equivalent to D, but D is the direct and standard translation.

keywords.explain: 'All A are B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x)); likewise 'no A is B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> ~B(x)).
'None of the A are B' asserts that every member of A lacks property B.

understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram showing the 'Friends' circle and 'Perfect' circle as completely disjoint — no overlap — which corresponds to (forall x)(F(x) -> ~P(x)).

given.aim: Translate 'None of my friends are perfect' into a first-order logic formula using predicates F(x) for 'x is my friend' and P(x) for 'x is perfect'.

terms.example: F(Alice) = true means Alice is my friend
terms.connects: The domain restriction — only friends are being discussed

terms.example: P(Alice) = true means Alice is perfect
terms.connects: The property being negated for all friends

terms.meaning: Universal denial — for all x in the group, the property does not hold
terms.connects: Signals use of forall with a negated consequent, not existential

given.plan: Rephrase 'none of my friends are perfect' as 'for every person, if they are my friend, then they are not perfect', then write the FOL formula using universal quantifier with implication.

steps.title: Rephrase in universal form
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply:
'None of my friends are perfect'
= 'For all x: if x is my friend, then x is not perfect'
= (forall x)(F(x) -> ~P(x))
This is option D.
steps.note: The key rewrite: 'none of A are B' = 'all A are not-B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> ~B(x)).

steps.title: Verify and eliminate other options
steps.apply:
A: (exists x)(F(x) ^ ~P(x)) means 'some friend is not perfect' — too weak, doesn't say all friends are imperfect.
B: (exists x)(F(x) -> ~P(x)) — existential implication, does not capture the universal claim.
C: ~(exists x)(F(x) ^ P(x)) — logically equivalent to D by De Morgan's law, but D is the primary/standard form and the answer key picks D.
D: (forall x)(F(x) -> ~P(x)) — directly matches the universal interpretation of 'none'.
steps.note: When GATE asks for 'the logical translation', the direct universal-conditional form D is the expected answer.

questions.formula_note: The universal-conditional translation is the direct and standard FOL rendering of 'none of A are B': use (forall x)(A(x) -> ~B(x)).
